topic: Theme 2: The UK Economy - Performance and Policies
subject: economics

items.correct: The change from one additional unit — marginal cost is the cost of producing one more unit; marginal utility is the satisfaction from one more unit. Marginal is incremental, not total or average.
items.misconception: Marginal cost is the total cost of producing all units.
items.why: 'Marginal' has a specific technical meaning: the next unit. Profit maximisation, optimal consumption and many other results depend on marginal — not total or average — quantities. Students conflate the three measures.

items.q: What is the technical definition of a recession?
items.correct: Two consecutive quarters of negative real GDP growth — a contraction of the economy.
items.misconception: A recession is when prices fall and people lose jobs.
items.random_wrong: A recession is when the stock market crashes.
items.why: Job losses and stock falls often accompany recessions but don't define them. The technical definition is GDP-based. Students conflate symptoms (unemployment) with definition (negative growth).

items.q: How is economic growth shown on a Production Possibility Curve?
items.correct: By an outward shift of the entire curve — reflecting an increase in productive capacity through more or better resources, technology, or skills.
items.misconception: Growth is shown by moving from inside the curve to a point on the curve.
items.random_wrong: Growth is shown by the curve becoming straighter.
items.why: Moving from inside to on the curve is RECOVERY (using existing resources better), not growth. True growth requires the curve to shift outward — students confuse recovery from inefficiency with new capacity.

items.q: The Laffer curve was popularised in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1974. “1954” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Solow's growth model shows long-run growth driven by:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Technological progress. “Capital accumulation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Solow publish his growth model?
items.why: Exact answer: 1956 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
